did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning (DL) pre-trained models, specifically DenseNet169 and VGG16, for skin cancer classification using dermoscopic images. It also employed explainable AI (XAI) techniques including occlusion, integrated gradients, and guided Grad-CAM to enhance model interpretability.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from fine-tuning DenseNet169 and VGG16 models on a dermoscopic image dataset, reporting specific performance metrics (e.g., 90.61% accuracy for DenseNet169) and applying XAI techniques. Although published in a conference proceedings (IEEE SSD), it contains original validation and technical study components rather than merely summarizing prior literature.

title: Explainable Pre-trained Models for Skin Cancer Classification
journal: 2025 IEEE 22nd International Multi-Conference on Systems, Signals & Devices (SSD)
brief_summary: This study investigated the effectiveness of pre-trained deep learning models, specifically DenseNet169 and VGG16, for classifying benign and malignant skin cancers using dermoscopic images from a public Kaggle dataset. The models were fine-tuned and evaluated based on accuracy, with DenseNet169 achieving the highest performance at 90.61% compared to VGG16's 84.24%. To address the opacity of deep learning models, three explainable AI (XAI) techniques—occlusion, integrated gradients, and guided Grad-CAM—were applied to enhance interpretability and highlight relevant image regions. The results demonstrate that DenseNet169 outperforms VGG16 in classification accuracy while providing reliable insights through XAI methods. These findings suggest that integrating XAI with high-performing pre-trained models can improve transparency and support clinical decision-making for dermatologists.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two binary classes used in the classification task on a Kaggle dataset. The dataset distinguishes between benign and malignant skin cancers.

One of the two binary classes used in the classification task. The paper mentions melanoma as a potentially fatal malignant skin cancer in the introduction and uses a dataset labeled 'Skin Cancer: Malignant vs. Benign'.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of dermoscopic images into benign or malignant skin cancer categories
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'binary classification to discriminate between benign and malignant skin cancers' using pre-trained models (DenseNet169 and VGG16) on a Kaggle dataset. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score, with DenseNet169 achieving 90.61% accuracy.

datasets_used.name: Skin Cancer: Malignant vs. Benign
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopic images available on Kaggle, used for binary classification of skin cancers. The dataset contains images classified into benign and malignant categories.

use.training: The dataset was split into training and test sets. The models were trained on the training portion of this dataset.
use.testing: The dataset was split into training and test sets. The models were evaluated on the test portion of this dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between DenseNet169 and VGG16 were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score) and confusion matrices without statistical significance testing.

Skin Cancer: Malignant vs. Benign (Kaggl.notes: DenseNet169 outperformed VGG16 across all evaluated metrics. The study also applied three XAI techniques (occlusion, integrated gradients, and guided Grad-CAM) to enhance interpretability, highlighting key regions influencing predictions.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained models (ImageNet pre-training implied by context of transfer learning for melanoma detection in related works, but explicitly stated as 'pre-trained' in the study title and abstract; specific pretraining dataset not explicitly named for the models used in the methodology section, though ImageNet is standard for these architectures and mentioned in related work context)

preprocessing: Data augmentation (vertical/horizontal flips, brightness/contrast adjustments, rotations up to 90 degrees)

conclusions: DenseNet169 outperformed VGG16 in binary classification of benign versus malignant skin cancers, achieving an accuracy of 90.61% compared to VGG16's 84.24%.
DenseNet169 demonstrated superior performance across all evaluated metrics (precision, recall, and F1 score) compared to VGG16.

The application of three explainable AI (XAI) techniques—occlusion, integrated gradients, and guided Grad-CAM—successfully highlighted key regions in input images that influenced classification decisions, thereby enhancing model interpretability.
Integrating XAI with high-performing pre-trained models improves transparency and confidence in AI predictions, which can support dermatologists in making informed clinical decisions.
While the study demonstrates the effectiveness of DL models for skin cancer classification, further research is needed to demonstrate their reliability in actual clinical settings.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, single public Kaggle dataset (660 images) without external validation, limiting generalizability.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to compare the performance of DenseNet169 and VGG16.
The paper explicitly notes that further research is needed to demonstrate the reliability of these models in actual clinical settings.
The dataset distribution and class balance are not explicitly detailed in the provided text, raising potential bias concerns.
The study is a conference paper with limited scope, focusing only on two architectures and binary classification.
Data leakage risks are not addressed; the split between training and test sets is mentioned but specific methodology (e.g., random vs. stratified, patient-level splitting) is not detailed.
